narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20—which is right around the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset—and your LSAT score of 31. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, assessed your overall chances and estimated an 84.2% probability that you would fail it. This high predicted risk of bar failure is why your application was rejected; the school uses this model to prioritize students with stronger projected odds of bar success, ensuring their graduates contribute to high pass rates. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which was the top factor pushing toward a failure prediction—far below the average 5.53 decile, signaling weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2nd, also well under the typical 5.71 average, which further reinforced the model's concern about your early academic trajectory. Your LSAT score rounded out the key influences, as its lower-than-average value of 31 compared to the usual 36.70 nudged the prediction even more toward failure risk. These three academic indicators together painted a picture of inconsistent performance that the model sees as the biggest hurdles for students like you in ultimately passing the bar. While factors like your full-time status align with the vast majority of successful applicants, they weren't enough to offset these core concerns. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your decile rankings are notably lower, placing you in the bottom tiers where failure rates climb significantly. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal story, but it's how the model weighed the data patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or prep programs if possible, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and growth. Many applicants in similar situations reapply successfully after targeted improvements—your upper-middle background and full-time commitment give you a solid foundation to build on. Keep pushing; law school doors can reopen with these adjustments.
